title: 6 Best Amazon Marketplace Alternatives for Sellers in 2025
metaDescription: Amazon Marketplace fees and rules making selling harder? These 6 alternatives in 2025 give you more control, lower fees, and better seller experience.
heroImageAlt: A seller packing a box with items for online sale, surrounded by inventory on shelves with a laptop showing a marketplace dashboard
content:
6 Best Amazon Marketplace Alternatives for Sellers in 2025

Selling on Amazon is a bit like renting a stall in someone else's mall — a mall that can decide to open its own competing stall right next to yours and undercut you on price. The fees are layered, the policies are complex, and the playing field tilts toward Amazon's own products more every year. If you're a seller looking for platforms that actually put you first, here are six Amazon Marketplace alternatives worth your attention in 2025.

Amazon Marketplace vs. 6 Alternatives

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly 10% (max $20) Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best for Used Goods
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for resale reach
Etsy 6.5% + listing Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for handmade/vintage
Walmart Marketplace 6-20% referral Cash / card No Yes Account required Best Amazon alternative for new goods
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Best for local selling
Mercari 10% Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Simple & accessible

#1 Swapedly — The Best Amazon Alternative for Secondhand Selling

Amazon Marketplace is built for new goods, brand-name products, and professional inventory management. That makes it a poor fit for the seller who wants to move used electronics, secondhand furniture, vintage finds, or household items. Swapedly is specifically built for that world — and built well.

The fee comparison isn't even close. Amazon referral fees range from 8-15% depending on category, then you add FBA fees if you want Amazon to handle fulfillment, plus a $39.99/month professional seller subscription if you're serious about volume. On Swapedly, it's simple: 10% fee capped at $20, free to list, no subscription required. Sell a $400 item, pay $20 max. The end.

The bigger philosophical difference is seller control. On Amazon, your listing competes against Amazon's own products and the exact same product from 47 other sellers. On Swapedly, your listing is your listing. The Swap Bucks (SB) currency means buyers spend platform credit — they're not just choosing you over a cheaper Amazon listing. The competitive dynamic is entirely different.

Swapedly's ecosystem also rewards sellers directly: earn 1.5x SB with the Swapedly Plus membership at $9.99/month, get 5 featured listings, and benefit from a referral program that pays 1 SB per friend who signs up. New users get a $40 Swap Bucks gift card at signup — something Amazon has never offered any seller. Grab yours at swapedly.com.

For sellers who want local pickup options — which Amazon doesn't support at all — Swapedly handles that too. Seller's choice: local pickup, shipping, or both.

Swapedly Pros

  • 10% capped at $20 — far simpler and cheaper than Amazon
  • No monthly subscription required
  • $40 Swap Bucks for new users
  • No Amazon competing against your own listings
  • Local pickup and shipping both available
  • Privacy-first — no social media account required

Swapedly Cons

  • Best for used goods — not for new brand-name inventory
  • Swap Bucks can't be withdrawn as cash

#2 eBay — The Classic Amazon Alternative for Resellers

eBay's relationship with Amazon is almost as old as the internet itself — they've been direct competitors since the late 1990s. eBay's advantage remains its global buyer pool and auction capability for items where demand determines price. For resellers moving secondhand branded goods, collectibles, or specialty items, eBay's reach often justifies the 13.25% fee.

The key structural difference from Amazon: on eBay, you're selling your specific item, not a generic SKU. A seller listing a specific vintage camera is the only seller of that exact item. That uniqueness commands different pricing dynamics than Amazon's race-to-the-bottom commodity listings.

eBay Pros

  • Unique item listings — no competing sellers on the same product
  • Global buyer reach
  • Auction format for price discovery on rare items

eBay Cons

  • 13.25% fee — higher than most alternatives
  • Complex, intimidating for casual sellers
  • Returns policy significantly favors buyers

#3 Etsy — Best for Creative and Handmade Sellers

Amazon Handmade exists, but it exists on Amazon's terms with Amazon's 15% fee. Etsy was built for independent makers and charges 6.5% — significantly less. For handcrafted goods, vintage items, and craft supplies, Etsy's buyer base is specifically there to find unique goods, not commodity products. That audience intent translates to real sales.

Etsy's independence from Amazon's ecosystem is its biggest asset. Sellers build brands on Etsy, not just product listings. Reviews accumulate under your shop name. Buyers follow your shop, not just a product category.

Etsy Pros

  • 6.5% fee — lower than Amazon
  • Dedicated audience for handmade and vintage
  • Brand-building is possible — not just listing optimization

Etsy Cons

  • $0.20 per listing fee adds up
  • Not suitable for non-handmade or non-vintage goods
  • Saturated in popular categories

#4 Walmart Marketplace — The Closest Amazon Competitor

If you're a professional seller specifically seeking the closest equivalent to Amazon's scale, Walmart Marketplace is worth considering. It's the second-largest e-commerce platform in the US, it's growing fast, and it's actively courting third-party sellers with competitive fee structures (referral fees range from 6-20% by category) and dedicated seller support.

The approval process is more selective than Amazon — Walmart vets sellers and maintains higher product quality standards. That selectivity cuts both ways: fewer sellers means less competition for you if you get approved, but it also means it's harder to get started.

Walmart Marketplace Pros

  • Second-largest US e-commerce platform
  • Less seller competition than Amazon in many categories
  • Competitive referral fee rates in some categories

Walmart Marketplace Cons

  • Selective application process — not easy to get started
  • Only for new goods and established brands
  • Not suitable for secondhand selling

#5 Facebook Marketplace — Local Selling Without Amazon

For items that don't need to ship — think furniture, appliances, outdoor equipment — Facebook Marketplace is a free and effective alternative to Amazon's non-existent local selling capability. The buyer pool is enormous, local transactions cost nothing, and large items that Amazon can't practically sell are Facebook Marketplace's specialty.

The privacy concern aside, Facebook Marketplace fills a genuine gap for sellers who want to move physical goods locally without the complexity of Amazon's FBA system or the fees of shipped platforms.

Facebook Marketplace Pros

  • Free for local transactions
  • No shipping required for large items
  • Massive local buyer base

Facebook Marketplace Cons

  • Privacy concerns — real identity fully visible
  • Cash only locally — no payment protection
  • Scam accounts are a consistent problem

#6 Mercari — The Approachable Amazon Alternative

Mercari's appeal over Amazon as an alternative comes down to simplicity. Where Amazon requires a professional account, UPC codes, complex listing specifications, and brand registry considerations, Mercari asks you to take a few photos and write a description. That accessibility is genuinely valuable for casual and semi-regular sellers.

The 10% flat fee with no listing charge is predictable and fair. The shipping workflow is beginner-friendly. For sellers who tried Amazon, found it overwhelming, and want something that gets out of the way, Mercari delivers.

Mercari Pros

  • Dramatically simpler than Amazon
  • No listing fees, just 10% on sale
  • Beginner-friendly shipping integration

Mercari Cons

  • No fee cap — 10% on everything
  • Much smaller buyer pool than Amazon
  • Not suitable for high-volume professional selling

The Honest Verdict

Amazon Marketplace is a powerful tool for the right type of seller — professional, brand-focused, high-volume, new goods. For everyone else, it's an expensive, complex, and increasingly competitive environment where Amazon itself is your biggest competitor.

Swapedly is the best alternative for secondhand sellers: lower fees with a cap, no monthly subscription required, a currency system that keeps your earnings in productive use, and a platform that doesn't work against you. The $40 new user gift card is a genuine perk that no other platform matches.

For new goods at scale, Walmart Marketplace is worth exploring. For handmade goods, Etsy. For rare resale, eBay. But for everyday secondhand selling in 2025, Swapedly has the best combination of economics and user experience.
